texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Financial LLMs hold promise for advancing financial tasks and domain-specific applications. However, they are limited by scarce corpora, weak multimodal capabilities, and narrow evaluations, making them less suited for real-world application. To address this, we introduce Open-FinLLMs , the first open-source multimodal financial LLMs designed to handle diverse tasks across text, tabular, time-series, and chart data, excelling in zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuning settings. The suite includes FinLLaMA, pre-trained on a comprehensive 52billion-token corpus; FinLLaMA-Instruct, finetuned with 573K financial instructions; and FinLLaVA, enhanced with 1.43M multimodal tuning pairs for strong cross-modal reasoning. We comprehensively evaluate Open-FinLLMs across 14 financial tasks, 30 datasets, and 4 multimodal tasks in zero-shot, few-shot, and supervised fine-tuning settings, introducing two new multimodal evaluation datasets. Our results show that Open-FinLLMs outperforms afvanced financial and general LLMs such as GPT-4, across financial NLP, decision-making, and multi-modal tasks, highlighting their potential to tackle real-world challenges. To foster innovation and collaboration across academia and industry, we release all codes 1 and models under OSI-approved licenses.
texts.text: Financial LLMs hold promise for advancing financial tasks and domain-specific applications. However, they are limited by scarce corpora, weak multimodal capabilities, and narrow evaluations, making them less suited for real-world application. To address this, we introduce Open-FinLLMs , the first open-source multimodal financial LLMs designed to handle diverse tasks across text, tabular, time-series, and chart data, excelling in zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuning settings. The suite includes FinLLaMA, pre-trained on a comprehensive 52billion-token corpus; FinLLaMA-Instruct, finetuned with 573K financial instructions; and FinLLaVA, enhanced with 1.43M multimodal tuning pairs for strong cross-modal reasoning. We comprehensively evaluate Open-FinLLMs across 14 financial tasks, 30 datasets, and 4 multimodal tasks in zero-shot, few-shot, and supervised fine-tuning settings, introducing two new multimodal evaluation datasets. Our results show that Open-FinLLMs outperforms afvanced financial and general LLMs such as GPT-4, across financial NLP, decision-making, and multi-modal tasks, highlighting their potential to tackle real-world challenges. To foster innovation and collaboration across academia and industry, we release all codes 1 and models under OSI-approved licenses.

texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The advancements of financial AI have been significantly driven by the progress of natural language processing (NLP) techniques, particularly large language models (LLMs) (Brown et al., 2020; Bubeck et al., 2023). Commercial LLMs like OpenAI's GPT-4 (Achiam et al., 2023) and open-source LLMs such as Meta AI's LLaMA series (Touvron et al., 2023a,b) have set new benchmarks in NLP tasks and vertical-domain tasks like medicine, owing to their impressive text understanding and generation capabilities. However, these generalpurpose LLMs face limitations in the financial domain due to the knowledge gap. These models are primarily pretrained on general texts and lack an understanding of financial terminology, regulations, and market nuances (Xie et al., 2023a; Wu et al., 2023a; Xie et al., 2023b).
texts.text: The advancements of financial AI have been significantly driven by the progress of natural language processing (NLP) techniques, particularly large language models (LLMs) (Brown et al., 2020; Bubeck et al., 2023). Commercial LLMs like OpenAI's GPT-4 (Achiam et al., 2023) and open-source LLMs such as Meta AI's LLaMA series (Touvron et al., 2023a,b) have set new benchmarks in NLP tasks and vertical-domain tasks like medicine, owing to their impressive text understanding and generation capabilities. However, these generalpurpose LLMs face limitations in the financial domain due to the knowledge gap. These models are primarily pretrained on general texts and lack an understanding of financial terminology, regulations, and market nuances (Xie et al., 2023a; Wu et al., 2023a; Xie et al., 2023b).

Third , evaluations are conducted on narrow scenarios , mainly instruction-tuned financial NLP tasks. Zero/fewshot performance, multimodal reasoning, and financial decision-making tasks remain underexplored, limiting real-world applicability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To address current limitations, we introduce Open-FinLLMs , the first open-source multimodal financial LLMs capable of tackling a wide range of financial tasks across text, tabular, time-series, and chart data, excelling in zero-shot, few-shot, and supervised fine-tuning settings. We begin with FinLLaMA, a foundational model pre-trained on a massive 52-billion-token corpus combining text, tabular, and time-series data from high-quality financial sources like reports, papers, and market data. This multi-modal pre-training gives FinLLaMA strong financial insights and analytical skills. We further develop FinLLaMA-Instruct based on FinLLaMA, fine-tuned on 573K diverse, high-quality financial instructions for better instruction-following and broader domain coverage. We also present FinLLaVA, the first model to handle images, text, charts, and tables, trained on 1,430K multimodal financial instruction pairs based on FinLLaMA. Unlike prior models focused on image-text pairs, our fine-tuning incorporates tabular image layouts, improving multi-modal alignment and complex data processing.

We begin with FinLLaMA, a foundational model pre-trained on a massive 52-billion-token corpus combining text, tabular, and time-series data from high-quality financial sources like reports, papers, and market data. This multi-modal pre-training gives FinLLaMA strong financial insights and analytical skills. We further develop FinLLaMA-Instruct based on FinLLaMA, fine-tuned on 573K diverse, high-quality financial instructions for better instruction-following and broader domain coverage. We also present FinLLaVA, the first model to handle images, text, charts, and tables, trained on 1,430K multimodal financial instruction pairs based on FinLLaMA. Unlike prior models focused on image-text pairs, our fine-tuning incorporates tabular image layouts, improving multi-modal alignment and complex data processing.

texts.orig: In summary, our key contributions are 2 : (1) Introducing Open-FinLLMs, a suite of open-source financial LLMs trained on the largest and most diverse datasets, covering various financial sources and tasks. FinLLaVA is the first open-source model to handle text, tabular, time-series, and chart data. (2) Comprehensive evaluation on 14 financial tasks (30 datasets), 4 multimodal tasks, and three settings (zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuning), including two new financial multimodal benchmarks. texts.orig: 2.1 FinLLaMA: Specializing LLaMA3 for Finance with Continual Pre-training
texts.text: 2.1 FinLLaMA: Specializing LLaMA3 for Finance with Continual Pre-training

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Curation of Continual Pre-training Corpus. To facilitate effective continual pretraining, we construct a comprehensive financial corpus comprising 52 billion tokens sourced from seven diverse financial domains. As shown in Table 2, our continual pre-training corpus includes diverse financial data sources for comprehensive coverage: (1) Financial papers: 4 billion tokens from academic papers (2000-2023) on topics like market analysis and economic theory. (2) Conference calls: 5 billion tokens from earnings call transcripts (2004-2021), offering insights into corporate performance. (3) Financial reports: 5 billion tokens from annual and quarterly reports (2005-2020), key for assessing company health. (4) Technical indicators: 12 billion tokens from economic data (2009-2023), essential for investment decisions. (5) News and social media: 7 billion tokens from financial news and social media (1999-2021), providing real-time market trends. (6) Historical data: 13 billion tokens from stock prices and market data (1999-2022), critical for quantitative analysis. (7) SEC filings: 6 billion tokens from SEC reports (1994-2022), detailing corporate activities. Unlike other models, we avoid using web data due to its higher noise level. Further details are in Appendix B.
texts.text: Curation of Continual Pre-training Corpus. To facilitate effective continual pretraining, we construct a comprehensive financial corpus comprising 52 billion tokens sourced from seven diverse financial domains. As shown in Table 2, our continual pre-training corpus includes diverse financial data sources for comprehensive coverage: (1) Financial papers: 4 billion tokens from academic papers (2000-2023) on topics like market analysis and economic theory. (2) Conference calls: 5 billion tokens from earnings call transcripts (2004-2021), offering insights into corporate performance. (3) Financial reports: 5 billion tokens from annual and quarterly reports (2005-2020), key for assessing company health. (4) Technical indicators: 12 billion tokens from economic data (2009-2023), essential for investment decisions. (5) News and social media: 7 billion tokens from financial news and social media (1999-2021), providing real-time market trends. (6) Historical data: 13 billion tokens from stock prices and market data (1999-2022), critical for quantitative analysis. (7) SEC filings: 6 billion tokens from SEC reports (1994-2022), detailing corporate activities. Unlike other models, we avoid using web data due to its higher noise level. Further details are in Appendix B.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mixing Financial Data with General Data . To prevent catastrophic forgetting, as highlighted in previous studies (Wu et al., 2023a; Gupta et al.), we integrate a subset of general-domain data with our financial-domain corpus. We use the Fineweb dataset (Penedo et al., 2024), which contains over 15 trillion tokens of cleaned and deduplicated English web data from CommonCrawl. Using the approach outlined in DoReMi (Xie et al., 2024b), we determine the optimal mixture ratio of financial to general-domain data to be around 3:1. Accordingly, we sampled a total of 18 billion tokens from the general-domain corpus. This mixture helps our models retain previously learned general knowledge while being specifically fine-tuned for financial tasks.
texts.text: Mixing Financial Data with General Data . To prevent catastrophic forgetting, as highlighted in previous studies (Wu et al., 2023a; Gupta et al.), we integrate a subset of general-domain data with our financial-domain corpus. We use the Fineweb dataset (Penedo et al., 2024), which contains over 15 trillion tokens of cleaned and deduplicated English web data from CommonCrawl. Using the approach outlined in DoReMi (Xie et al., 2024b), we determine the optimal mixture ratio of financial to general-domain data to be around 3:1. Accordingly, we sampled a total of 18 billion tokens from the general-domain corpus. This mixture helps our models retain previously learned general knowledge while being specifically fine-tuned for financial tasks.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pretraining Details. We use LLaMA3-8B as the backbone, optimizing the token sequence likelihood with a standard language modeling objective. The model maximizes L (Θ) = ∑ k i log P Θ ( x i | x 1 , . . . , x i -1 ) to predict token sequences. Distributed training is performed using DeepSpeed on 64 A100 80GB GPUs across 8 nodes, requiring 250 hours for 1 epoch. The learning rate is set to 1 × 10 -5 with a cosine schedule, weight decay of 0.00001, and a 0.05 warm-up ratio. The batch size is 2 per device, with a maximum sequence length of 8,192 tokens.

texts.orig: 2.2 FinLLaMA-Instruct: Domain Task Optimization through Instruction Tuning
texts.text: 2.2 FinLLaMA-Instruct: Domain Task Optimization through Instruction Tuning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Building on the foundation of FinLLaMA, we propose FinLLaMA-Instruct-8B, developed through instruction fine-tuning to enhance the model's instruction-following capabilities and optimize performance on downstream domain tasks.
texts.text: Building on the foundation of FinLLaMA, we propose FinLLaMA-Instruct-8B, developed through instruction fine-tuning to enhance the model's instruction-following capabilities and optimize performance on downstream domain tasks.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Financial Instruction Dataset To optimize FinLLaMA for downstream domain tasks and instruction following ability, we have assembled an extensive instruction-tuning dataset, totaling 573K samples, specifically tailored for financial applications, as shown in Table 3. The dataset is sourced from four open data sources: (1) FLUPE (Xie et al., 2023b), with 123K samples covering key financial NLP tasks; (2) finred (Liu et al., 2023), with ap- proximately 32.67K examples focused on financial report and document comprehension; (3) MathInstruct (Yue et al., 2023), with 262K examples from 13 distinct mathematical rationale datasets; and (4) Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k, which integrates data from 18 different financial NLP datasets. To ensure the uniqueness and quality of the data, we performed rigorous deduplication and filtering, addressing potential overlaps between datasets like FLUPE and Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k. See Appendix C for more details on the instruction tuning data and data processing.
texts.text: Financial Instruction Dataset To optimize FinLLaMA for downstream domain tasks and instruction following ability, we have assembled an extensive instruction-tuning dataset, totaling 573K samples, specifically tailored for financial applications, as shown in Table 3. The dataset is sourced from four open data sources: (1) FLUPE (Xie et al., 2023b), with 123K samples covering key financial NLP tasks; (2) finred (Liu et al., 2023), with ap- proximately 32.67K examples focused on financial report and document comprehension; (3) MathInstruct (Yue et al., 2023), with 262K examples from 13 distinct mathematical rationale datasets; and (4) Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k, which integrates data from 18 different financial NLP datasets. To ensure the uniqueness and quality of the data, we performed rigorous deduplication and filtering, addressing potential overlaps between datasets like FLUPE and Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k. See Appendix C for more details on the instruction tuning data and data processing.

texts.orig: Instrution-tuning Details. For instruction tuning, we utilize FinLLaMA as the backbone model and conduct training on 8 NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs for 6 hours. The model is optimized using Qlora (Dettmers et al., 2024) via AutoTrain 3 , configured with a block size and model maximum length of 4096. We train the model over 2 epochs with a batch size of 1 and a learning rate of 0.0002. Parameter-efficient tuning is achieved with LoRA settings of r = 64 , α = 128 , and no dropout, using INT4 quantization. All linear modules are targeted, with right-aligned padding. The AdamW optimizer (Loshchilov and Hutter), coupled with a cosine scheduler, is used for optimization, along with gradient accumulation set to 4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Multimodal Instruction Finetuning. We use CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) as the visual encoder and FinLLaMA as the language decoder, finetuning on our multimodal instruction dataset. Our approach follows LLaVA-1.5's framework (Liu et al., 2024b), using a two-stage instruction-tuning process: (1) Multimodal Alignment: We align the vision encoder's output with the language model's embedding space by freezing both model weights and training a two-layer MLP projector to map visual features into the language model's space. The training objective maximizes the auto-regressive likelihood: ∑ L i P θ ( X i | X v , X instruct ,<i , X a,<i ) . (2) Supervised Fine-tuning: In this stage, we update both the language model and MLP projector while keeping the vision encoder frozen, applying the same objective to a new dataset, primarily LLaVA-v1.5-mix665k and Evol-Instruct.
texts.text: Multimodal Instruction Finetuning. We use CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) as the visual encoder and FinLLaMA as the language decoder, finetuning on our multimodal instruction dataset. Our approach follows LLaVA-1.5's framework (Liu et al., 2024b), using a two-stage instruction-tuning process: (1) Multimodal Alignment: We align the vision encoder's output with the language model's embedding space by freezing both model weights and training a two-layer MLP projector to map visual features into the language model's space. The training objective maximizes the auto-regressive likelihood: ∑ L i P θ ( X i | X v , X instruct ,<i , X a,<i ) . (2) Supervised Fine-tuning: In this stage, we update both the language model and MLP projector while keeping the vision encoder frozen, applying the same objective to a new dataset, primarily LLaVA-v1.5-mix665k and Evol-Instruct.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Training Details: In the multi-modal alignment stage, we set the global batch size to 128, the learning rate to 1 × 10 -3 , with a warm-up ratio of 0.03 and cosine decay. Training uses bf16 and tf32 precision for stability and acceleration. Weight decay is set to 0.0, and the model's maximum length is 2048 tokens. We train on eight NVIDIA HGX H20 GPUs, completing one epoch in approximately 30 hours. In the SFT stage, we set the global batch size to 256, the learning rate to 2 × 10 -5 , with a warmup ratio of 0.05 and cosine decay. The model's maximum length is increased to 8192 tokens for longer sequences. Weight decay remains at 0.0, and training runs for one epoch with the same precision settings for efficiency and performance.

texts.orig: Zero-shot and Few-shot Performance We evaluate FinLLaMA on both zero-shot and few-shot settings. In the zero-shot setting (Table 8, Appendix F.1), we assess 19 datasets across financialNLP and financial-specific tasks (Appendix F.4.1, F.4.2). As shown in Table 5 and Figure 5 (Appendix F.4.2), FinLLaMA consistently outperforms baselines , surpassing LLaMA3-8B on all tasks and outperforming BloombergGPT (50B) and LLaMA3.1-8B on most, demonstrating strong zero-shot capabilities from domain-specific continual pre-training. It excels in sentiment analysis, classification, fact-checking (FinFact), and math tasks (MC, KnowledgeMath, DocMath-Eval). In credit scoring, performance varies: on the German dataset, FinLLaMA and LLaMA3-8B predicted one class entirely, while LLaMA3.1-8B predicted the opposite; on the Australian dataset, FinLLaMA performs best; on LendingClub, it beats LLaMA38B but not LLaMA3.1-8B. It also shows strong results in fraud detection, financial distress identification, and claim analysis.

texts.orig: In the few-shot setting, we evaluate four datasets across three financial-NLP tasks (Table 9, Appendix F.4.2). As shown in Figure 6, FinLLaMA again outperforms all baselines . In NER, it achieves an F1 of 82.10, surpassing LLaMA3-8B (39.18), LLaMA3.1-8B (49.04), and BloombergGPT (60.82). On FPB, it scores 70.25, outperforming LLaMA3-8B (69.65), LLaMA3.18B (13.08), and BloombergGPT (51.07). On FiQASA, it achieves 75.34 vs. LLaMA3-8B (52.29), LLaMA3.1-8B (65.39), and BloombergGPT (75.05). On Headlines, it reaches 85.54, outperforming LLaMA3-8B (80.59), LLaMA3.1-8B (59.95), and BloombergGPT (82.20), showing strong classification performance even with minimal examples.

texts.orig: Agent We evaluate FinLLaMA on a financialspecific decision-making task using the FinMem framework (Yu et al., 2024), tested on a singleasset trading dataset from August 15, 2021, to April 25, 2023 (Table 12). This task measures Cumulative Return and Sharpe Ratio as key metrics (Appendix G). As shown in Table 12, FinLLaMA outperforms other LLMs with positive Cumulative Return and Sharpe Ratio, demonstrating profitability in dynamic trading environments. It achieves the highest Sharpe Ratio (above 1), indicating superior risk-return balance. FinLLaMA also maintains stability with an Annual Volatility of 0.4766 and a Maximum Drawdown of 0.2693-both lower than other models. This combination of high Sharpe Ratio and low volatility highlights FinLLaMA's ability to deliver consistent returns with minimized risk, underscoring the benefits of domain-specific continual pre-training. Detailed results are in Appendix H.

texts.orig: Multimodal Tasks We evaluate our model on four multimodal understanding tasks (Table 11). The MMMU-Overall dataset (10,500 instances) assesses general multimodal capabilities, while MMMU-Business (1,428 instances) focuses on financial domains such as accounting and marketing. In addition to existing datasets, we introduce two new financial multimodal benchmarks. ChartBench tests chart interpretation skills with 350 finance-related instances selected from ChartInstructionData. We used GPT-4o to assign financerelevance scores and selected samples scoring 9 or above (out of 10), categorized into seven groups (Appendix F.3), with 50 randomly sampled instances per category. TableBench evaluates multimodal reasoning over tabular images, providing a realistic setting for complex financial data interpretation. It contains 450 questions across comparison and data retrieval tasks, reflecting key decisionmaking processes in finance.

texts.orig: Performance As shown in Table 6, our multimodal model, FinLLaVA , achieves the best performance across all tasks among open-source 7B and 13B models, even surpassing larger models like LLaVA-1.5 and LLaVA-1.6 (Vicuna-13B). On TableBench , FinLLaVA not only outperforms open-source baselines but also surpasses commercial state-of-the-art models such as GPT-4 and Gemini-1.5-pro , demonstrating the effectiveness of our multimodal extension for complex financial data. These results highlight FinLLaVA's robustness and generalization, especially given that our evaluation uses out-of-domain data, unlike FinTral. By incorporating extensive OCR data during alignment, FinLLaVA improves its ability to interpret financial tables. With fully image-based inputs, users can directly provide table or chart images, making it practical for real-world applications like financial reporting and auditing. This simplicity enables financial professionals to streamline work- flows and enhance productivity without technical expertise. Overall, FinLLaVA achieves state-of-theart performance across multimodal financial tasks, surpassing both open-source and commercial models, with strong generalization and practical utility for real-world applications like financial reporting and auditing.

texts.orig: 4 Conclusion
texts.text: 4 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we present Open-FinLLMs, an innovative suite of open-source financial language models specifically designed to address the limitations of LLMs in financial applications. Our contributions include FinLLaMA, a foundational model built on the continual pre-training of LLaMA3 8B with an extensive multimodal domainspecific datasets, FinLLaMA-Instruct, fine-tuned with diverse instructions for improved instructionfollowing and conversational capabilities, and FinLLaVA, the multimodal extension capable of handling vision, tabular, and charts. Our comprehensive evaluation, which covers 14 financial tasks with 30 datasets and 4 multimodal tasks, demonstrates that Open-FinLLMs models outperform existing financial LLMs and even better performance to GPT-4 and GPT-4o despite their smaller size, on financial NLP, reasoning, decision-making and multimodal tasks in zero-shot, few-shot, and supervised fine-tuning settings. The results underscore the potential of Open-FinLLMs in advancing Financial AI applications through their robust performance in various financial tasks.
texts.text: In this paper, we present Open-FinLLMs, an innovative suite of open-source financial language models specifically designed to address the limitations of LLMs in financial applications. Our contributions include FinLLaMA, a foundational model built on the continual pre-training of LLaMA3 8B with an extensive multimodal domainspecific datasets, FinLLaMA-Instruct, fine-tuned with diverse instructions for improved instructionfollowing and conversational capabilities, and FinLLaVA, the multimodal extension capable of handling vision, tabular, and charts. Our comprehensive evaluation, which covers 14 financial tasks with 30 datasets and 4 multimodal tasks, demonstrates that Open-FinLLMs models outperform existing financial LLMs and even better performance to GPT-4 and GPT-4o despite their smaller size, on financial NLP, reasoning, decision-making and multimodal tasks in zero-shot, few-shot, and supervised fine-tuning settings. The results underscore the potential of Open-FinLLMs in advancing Financial AI applications through their robust performance in various financial tasks.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Limitation
texts.text: Limitation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our Open-FinLLMs, including the foundational FinLLaMA, the instruction-tuned FinLLaMAInstruct, and the multimodal FinLLaVA, demonstrate promising capabilities but have several limitations. First, the models are currently limited to a size of 8B parameters, and future work should explore both smaller models for efficiency and larger models for enhanced performance. Second, our experiments focused exclusively on English, highlighting the need to expand to multilingual settings to better serve global financial markets. Third, the tasks we addressed, such as trading-related scenarios, represent only a subset of potential applications, and future research should investigate broader industrial use cases like financial auditing, risk management, and regulatory compliance. Finally, the multimodal capabilities of FinLLaVA are restricted to handling charts and tabular data; incorporating other data types.

texts.text: Recently, several financial LLMs have been developed to address domain-specific challenges. BloombergGPT (Wu et al., 2023a) pioneered financial LLMs with pretraining on 363 billion financial tokens; however, it remains closed-source, limiting its accessibility. On the open-source front, PIXIU (Xie et al., 2023b) and FinGPT (Liu et al., 2023, 2024c; Yang et al., 2023) fine-tuned LLaMA

texts.label: text
texts.orig: models with hundreds of thousands of financial instructions, focusing primarily on text-based tasks. FinTral (Bhatia et al., 2024) introduced multimodal capabilities for tabular data, but limited by the small size of its domain-specific data and its evaluation scope. Fin-o1 (Qian et al., 2025), developed through instruction tuning on reasoning-enhanced data, demonstrates that domain-enhanced reasoning abilities significantly improve the model's performance in reasoning tasks.

texts.orig: A.2 Domain Specialization of Large Language Models
texts.text: A.2 Domain Specialization of Large Language Models

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Domain-specific LLMs are developed using three main strategies: pre-training from scratch (PT), continued pre-training (CPT), and instruction finetuning (SFT) (Wu et al., 2024). CPT adapts existing LLMsbyfurther training on domain data, like Code LLaMA (Roziere et al., 2023), which improves code generation. SFT tailors models for specific tasks with domain instructions, requiring less data and compute, as demonstrated by PIXIU (Xie et al., 2023b) and FinGPT (Liu et al., 2024c).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B.1.1 Data Processing & Cleaning
texts.text: B.1.1 Data Processing & Cleaning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In our data preprocessing pipeline for training Open-FinLLMs, we utilize Data-Juicer (Chen et al., 2024a) to clean and standardize the datasets. For each corpus, we remove email addresses and URLs to enhance privacy and reduce noise, ensuring the focus remains on the textual content. We address unicode inconsistencies by standardizing characters across the dataset, which maintains uniformity and aids in accurate text representation. Punctuation is normalized to provide consistency in text parsing, while excess whitespace is removed to improve readability and structure. For tabular and time-series data, we first split them into rows into samples of approximately 2,048 tokens each, formatting each block in HTML and ensuring each includes the table header for context. We then combine all datasets and further chunk the entire dataset into 8,192 token blocks, readying the data for efficient processing by the model.
texts.text: In our data preprocessing pipeline for training Open-FinLLMs, we utilize Data-Juicer (Chen et al., 2024a) to clean and standardize the datasets. For each corpus, we remove email addresses and URLs to enhance privacy and reduce noise, ensuring the focus remains on the textual content. We address unicode inconsistencies by standardizing characters across the dataset, which maintains uniformity and aids in accurate text representation. Punctuation is normalized to provide consistency in text parsing, while excess whitespace is removed to improve readability and structure. For tabular and time-series data, we first split them into rows into samples of approximately 2,048 tokens each, formatting each block in HTML and ensuring each includes the table header for context. We then combine all datasets and further chunk the entire dataset into 8,192 token blocks, readying the data for efficient processing by the model.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In our instruction dataset, we identified overlapping task samples between the FLUPE and Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k datasets, particularly in tasks such as sentiment analysis and NER. To address this, we manually excluded these redundant samples, which resulted in the removal of approximately 30,000 samples. This step was crucial to ensure that each task is represented uniquely and effectively in the dataset, avoiding any biases that could arise from duplicated entries.

texts.text: Make Comparisons : This category involves comparing different financial metrics or data points across various time periods, companies, or financial instruments. This is particularly useful in scenarios like peak revenue analysis or cost-cutting strategies.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Sentiment analysis focuses on extracting sentiment information (positive, negative, or neutral) from financial texts, using the TSA dataset.
texts.text: Sentiment analysis focuses on extracting sentiment information (positive, negative, or neutral) from financial texts, using the TSA dataset.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Classfication : 1) Hawkish-Dovish classification aims to classify sentences from monetary policy texts as 'hawkish' or 'dovish' focusing on the nuanced language and economic implications of financial texts, using the FOMC dataset. 2) Argument unit classification categorizes sentences as claims or premises, using the FinArg AUC dataset. 3) Deal completeness classification predicts if mergers and acquisitions events are "completed" or remain "rumors" based on news and tweets, employing the MA dataset.
texts.text: Classfication : 1) Hawkish-Dovish classification aims to classify sentences from monetary policy texts as 'hawkish' or 'dovish' focusing on the nuanced language and economic implications of financial texts, using the FOMC dataset. 2) Argument unit classification categorizes sentences as claims or premises, using the FinArg AUC dataset. 3) Deal completeness classification predicts if mergers and acquisitions events are "completed" or remain "rumors" based on news and tweets, employing the MA dataset.

texts.label: list_item

texts.label: list_item

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this task, at each time step, the LLM receives a feed of memories retrieved from the memory module. Based on this information, the LLM must make an investment decision-choosing to buy, sell, or hold-while providing its reasoning for the decision and specifying the index of the supporting information within the memory module. This process evaluates the model's ability to analyze financial data, make informed decisions, and justify its choices by referencing specific pieces of stored information in a dynamic trading environment.
